stem: Nitroglycerin given by infusion
explanation: GTN: NO donor causing veno- and arterio-dilation; lowers preload/afterload; impairs HPV; can cause headache, methaemoglobinaemia, raised ICP.

statements.explanation: Nitroglycerin attenuates hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction → can worsen V/Q.
Cerebral vasodilation raises ICP.
High doses produce methaemoglobinaemia.
No direct neuromuscular blockade interaction.
Direct effect is vasodilation, not myocardial contractility.

stem: Vasoactive drugs that exert a positive chronotropic effect on the heart include
explanation: Positive chronotropy: isoprenaline, ephedrine, dobutamine, adrenaline. Pure α-agonists (phenylephrine, metaraminol) cause reflex bradycardia.

statements.explanation: Metaraminol is mainly α-agonist; reflex BRADYCARDIA, not chronotropy.
Ephedrine is indirect sympathomimetic with both α and β1 → tachycardia.
Isoprenaline is non-selective β-agonist → strong chronotropy.
Phenylephrine pure α1 → reflex bradycardia.
Noradrenaline has β1 activity at the heart → can increase HR but reflex slowing common.

stem: Cerebral blood flow is inversely related to
explanation: CBF determinants: PaCO2 (direct), CMRO2 coupling (direct), autoregulation to MAP 50–150 mmHg, anaesthetic effects (dose-dependent).

statements.explanation: Isoflurane reduces CBF dose-dependently at low MAC; higher MAC causes vasodilation. Conservative answer: inversely related at low concentrations → TRUE.
CBF is DIRECTLY (not inversely) related to PaCO2 in normal range.
CBF rises only when PaO2 < 50 mmHg; normal range = independent (not inversely related).
CBF is coupled to CMRO2 — directly related (this is flow-metabolism coupling).
CBF is autoregulated to MAP 50–150 mmHg; outside range CBF tracks MAP directly.

stem: The purpose of an isolation transformer in the operating room is to
explanation: Isolation transformer: creates floating (un-earthed) secondary circuit; combined with line isolation monitor → safer theatre electrical supply.

statements.explanation: Earth-fault detector (separate device) detects shorts; isolation transformer creates the isolated circuit.
Isolation transformer creates an UNEARTHED secondary circuit → contact with one live wire doesn’t cause shock.

statements.text: boost the line voltage to power electrical equipment
statements.explanation: It transforms 1:1 — doesn’t boost voltage.
It prevents direct earthing of equipment.
Doesn’t reduce line current.

stem: Concerning benzodiazepines
explanation: Benzodiazepines: positive allosteric modulators of GABA_A, hepatic metabolism, flumazenil reverses, reduce CBF and CMRO2.

statements.text: they do not undergo hepatic biotransformation
statements.explanation: Benzodiazepines undergo hepatic biotransformation (mostly CYP3A4).

statements.text: their sedative effect can be reversed by physostigmine
statements.explanation: Flumazenil (not physostigmine) is the specific antagonist.

statements.text: they enhance the effect of GABA
statements.explanation: Enhance GABA effect by allosteric modulation at GABA_A.
Reduce CBF.
Reduce CMRO2.

stem: Concerning normal hepatic cellular function
explanation: Hepatocyte: smooth ER hosts CYP450 drug metabolism; mitochondria provide oxidative phosphorylation. Centrilobular zone has lowest PO2.

statements.text: the mitochondrial PO2 is less than 10 mm Hg
statements.explanation: Hepatocyte mitochondrial PO2 is ~5–10 mmHg (low intracellular gradient).

statements.text: drug metabolism is primarily a function of the mitochondria
statements.explanation: Drug metabolism is in the smooth endoplasmic reticulum (microsomal), not mitochondria.

statements.text: cytochrome P450 is essential for hydroxylation and dealkylation in drug metabolism
statements.explanation: CYP450 enzymes catalyse phase-I oxidations.

statements.text: the smooth endoplasmic reticulum hypertrophies when enzyme induction occurs
statements.explanation: SER hypertrophies with enzyme induction (e.g. phenobarbital).

statements.text: the mitochondria contain enzymes essential to the respiratory chain
statements.explanation: Mitochondria house the respiratory chain enzymes.

stem: Deviation from Beer's law may occur
explanation: Beer’s law assumes monochromatic light, dilute non-fluorescent solute, no interactions; deviations occur with broad-band filters, fluorescence, photodecomposition.

statements.text: when light from other species is absorbed at the same wavelength as that being measured
statements.explanation: Overlapping absorption from other species causes positive deviation.

statements.text: when there is interaction between solute and solvent
statements.explanation: Solute-solvent interactions alter molar absorption.

statements.text: more with gelatin filters than with narrow bandpass monochromators
statements.explanation: Gelatin filters have broad bandpass → more deviation than monochromators.

statements.text: when measuring the absorption of a fluorescent solution
statements.explanation: Fluorescent solutions emit light, distorting measurement.
Photo-decomposition changes concentration during measurement.

stem: The following are features of the cough associated with drugs that inhibit angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE):
explanation: ACE-inhibitor cough: bradykinin-mediated class effect; doesn’t resolve with another ACE-I; switch to ARB. More common in women.

statements.text: it improves on changing to a different ACE inhibitor
statements.explanation: ACE-inhibitor cough is a class effect — switching to another ACE-I doesn’t resolve it.
Dose-related component exists (some patients improve with dose reduction).

statements.text: it is more likely to occur when treating hypertension.
statements.explanation: Cough prevalence is similar across indications.

statements.text: it is more common in patients who smoke cigarettes
statements.explanation: Smokers may have other cough causes; ACE cough isn’t more common in them.

statements.text: it is more common in men than women
statements.explanation: Cough is MORE common in women than men.

stem: The binding of a drug to plasma proteins
explanation: Plasma-protein binding: albumin (acidic drugs), α1-acid glycoprotein (basic drugs); restricts glomerular filtration of bound drug.

statements.text: involves over 100 binding sites per albumin molecule
statements.explanation: Albumin has 2 main drug-binding sites (Sudlow I and II) — not 100.

statements.text: is markedly different in arterial and venous blood
statements.explanation: Plasma-protein binding is similar in arterial and venous blood.

statements.text: prevents rapid renal glomerular filtration of the drug
statements.explanation: Highly bound drugs are not filtered at glomerulus (only free fraction).
High binding prolongs half-life by reducing free fraction available for clearance.
Hepatic uptake may be reduced for high-extraction drugs that aren’t flow-limited.

stem: Spontaneous ventilation with a Mapleson A (Magill) breathing system
explanation: Mapleson A (Magill): spontaneous ventilation FGF = alveolar ventilation; inefficient for controlled ventilation (needs 2.5× MV). Reverse is D/E.

statements.text: functions efficiently with a fresh gas flow lower than alveolar ventilation
statements.explanation: Magill (A) is efficient for spontaneous ventilation — FGF can be approximately equal to MV.

statements.text: requires a fresh gas flow in excess of minute ventilation
statements.explanation: For spontaneous breathing FGF need only equal alveolar ventilation (~70 mL/kg/min), not exceed MV.

statements.text: results in less rebreathing than controlled ventilation using identical fresh gas flows
statements.explanation: Mapleson A is more efficient for spontaneous than controlled ventilation.

statements.text: delivers a tidal volume greater than the volumetric displacement of the bag
statements.explanation: Tidal volume = patient effort; bag size doesn’t limit it.
Open ends and APL valves allow air entrainment if FGF inadequate.

stem: Accidental burns associated with the use of diathermy may result from
explanation: Diathermy burns: faulty return electrode, alternative ground paths, capacitive coupling, hot bowel/instrument contact; bipolar diathermy reduces risk.

statements.text: simultaneous use of an isolated ECG monitor
statements.explanation: Isolated ECG monitor doesn’t cause diathermy burns.
Faulty indifferent (return) electrode focuses current → burn.
High current density at a small contact area causes burns.
Earthing the operating table is no longer modern practice but is not the principal cause.

statements.text: leakage current induced by capacitive coupling
statements.explanation: Capacitive coupling can induce current in nearby leads → burns.

stem: Recognised effects of acetylsalicylic acid include
explanation: Aspirin: irreversible COX inhibitor → antiplatelet, anti-inflammatory, antipyretic, gastric mucosal damage; toxic doses uncouple oxidative phosphorylation.

statements.explanation: Aspirin uncouples oxidative phosphorylation in toxicity (fever, lactic acidosis).
Inhibits thromboxane A2 production (antiplatelet effect).
Stimulates respiratory centre → respiratory alkalosis early.
Gastric mucosal irritation via COX-1 inhibition.
Inhibits prostaglandin synthesis via COX inhibition.

stem: Measurement of cardiac output by thermodilution is accurate if the
explanation: Thermodilution CO inaccurate with intracardiac shunts (ASD, VSD, ToF) and significant valvular regurgitation. Mitral stenosis itself doesn’t affect it.

statements.explanation: Fallot’s tetralogy has R→L shunt → inaccurate thermodilution.
Mitral stenosis doesn’t involve a shunt → thermodilution remains accurate.
Significant aortic incompetence introduces error in transpulmonary thermodilution.

statements.text: measurement is made using saline at room temperature
statements.explanation: Room-temperature saline can be used (less signal but acceptable with monitor compensation).

statements.text: patient has an intraventricular septal defect
statements.explanation: VSD introduces shunt → inaccurate thermodilution (loss/recirculation of indicator).

stem: In a normal subject, cerebral blood flow is increased by
explanation: CBF rises with: hypercapnia (PaCO2 >40), hypoxaemia <50 mmHg, head-down position, raised CMRO2. Falls with hypocapnia and hypothermia.

statements.explanation: Hyperventilation lowers PaCO2 → cerebral vasoconstriction → DECREASES CBF.

statements.text: an increase in systolic arterial blood pressure from 110 to 130 mmHg
statements.explanation: Within autoregulation range (50–150 mmHg) MAP change doesn’t change CBF.

statements.text: an increase in arterial PCO2 above 60 mmHg (8.5 kPa)
statements.explanation: PaCO2 > 60 mmHg → marked vasodilation → ↑CBF.
Head-down position increases jugular pressure → ↑ICP and CBV.

statements.text: a reduction in arterial PO2 to 70 mmHg (9.5 kPa)
statements.explanation: PaO2 <50 mmHg → hypoxic cerebral vasodilation; 70 mmHg is borderline.

stem: Normal intracellular electrolyte concentrations include
explanation: Intracellular electrolytes: K ~150, Na ~10, Cl ~5, phosphate high, bicarbonate ~10–15 mmol/L. Reverse gradients for sodium and potassium.

statements.explanation: Intracellular phosphate ~75 mmol/L (statement of "S" likely 75) — accept TRUE within typical range.
Intracellular Na ~10 mmol/L (extracellular ~140).
Intracellular K ~150 mmol/L.
Intracellular Cl ~5 mmol/L; 50 mmol/L too high.
Intracellular bicarbonate ~10–15 mmol/L.

stem: In the measurement of gas flow
explanation: Rotameter: laminar (density-dependent at low flow), turbulent (viscosity-dependent at high flow). Wright respirometer over-reads high flows, under-reads low.

statements.text: the accuracy of a rotameter, at high flows is most affected by changes in the viscosity of the gas
statements.explanation: At high flow rotameter accuracy depends on viscosity (turbulent flow).

statements.text: the accuracy of a rotameter at low flows is most affected by changes in the density of the gas
statements.explanation: At low flow rotameter accuracy depends on density (laminar flow).

statements.text: turbine respirometers of the Wright's variety over read at high flows
statements.explanation: Wright respirometer UNDER-reads at low flows and OVER-reads at high — statement of OVER-read at HIGH true. Both statements often combined.

statements.text: the accuracy of a rotameter is within 5%
statements.explanation: Rotameter accuracy ~±2.5–5%.

statements.text: turbine respirometers of the Wright's variety under read at low flows
statements.explanation: Wright UNDER-reads at low flows (turbine inertia).

stem: Blood flow to the thoracic spinal cord is
explanation: Thoracic spinal cord supply: anterior spinal artery from vertebrals + radicular branches from intercostals (Adamkiewicz, T8–L2); autoregulated, CO2-reactive.

statements.text: absent during cross clamping of the thoracic aorta
statements.explanation: Spinal cord ischaemia occurs during aortic cross-clamping.

statements.text: derived in part from the vertebral arteries
statements.explanation: Vertebral arteries contribute to upper spinal cord, anterior spinal artery feeds anterior cord.

statements.text: derived in part from the intercostal arteries
statements.explanation: Intercostal arteries (including artery of Adamkiewicz) supply thoracic cord.
Spinal cord blood flow is autoregulated AND responds to hypercarbia (CO2 reactivity).
Local autoregulation does occur in spinal cord vasculature.

stem: Glucagon administration increases
explanation: Glucagon: hyperglycaemic hormone via gluconeogenesis; cardiac stimulant via cAMP (used in β-blocker toxicity); side effects nausea, hyperglycaemia.

statements.explanation: Glucagon has positive inotropic effects (via adenyl cyclase, β-bypass).

statements.text: the excitability of the sinus node
statements.explanation: Increases sinus node excitability (positive chronotropy).
Activates adenylate cyclase → ↑cAMP.
Nausea is a recognised side effect.
Raises intracellular cardiac calcium via cAMP.

stem: The rate at which the alveolar concentration of sevoflurane will reach the inspired concentration is influenced by the
explanation: Alveolar/inspired sevo ratio rises faster with: high MV, low CO, low blood:gas solubility, low FRC, N2O second-gas effect.

statements.explanation: Alveolar minute ventilation directly speeds wash-in.

statements.text: use of carbon dioxide absorber in circuit
statements.explanation: CO2 absorber doesn’t affect alveolar concentration rise.
Oil:gas determines potency, not wash-in speed (blood:gas does).
High pulmonary blood flow uptakes more agent, SLOWING alveolar rise.
N2O second-gas effect speeds wash-in of sevoflurane.

stem: Features of lidocaine (lignocaine) toxicity include
explanation: Lidocaine toxicity progression: tongue/circumoral paraesthesia → tinnitus → twitching → seizures → CNS depression → cardiovascular collapse.

statements.explanation: Muscle twitching is an early CNS toxicity sign.
Dysarthria/speech difficulty occurs in mild toxicity.
Drowsiness/sedation is a CNS effect.
Circumoral tingling and tongue numbness are classic prodromes.
Tinnitus is a classic CNS warning of lidocaine toxicity.

stem: Recognised effects of clinical doses of meperidine (pethidine) include
explanation: Pethidine: weak μ-agonist, atropine-like effects (tachycardia, mydriasis), histamine release, active metabolite norpethidine causes seizures.

statements.explanation: Pethidine has atropine-like (mAChR) effects causing tachycardia.
Sedation is a recognised effect.
Pethidine releases histamine.
Direct myocardial depression at clinical doses is minimal; high doses can depress.
Pethidine does NOT antagonise opioid effects (it is an agonist).

stem: Mechanical hyperventilation to a PaCO2 of 3kPa (22.5mm Hg) causes
explanation: Hyperventilation/hypocapnia: cerebral and peripheral vasoconstriction, low CO, low K+, low iCa (tetany), leftward ODC shift.

statements.explanation: Hypocapnia causes peripheral and pulmonary vasoconstriction → reduced venous return → CO falls.

statements.text: a reduction in serum potassium concentration
statements.explanation: Alkalosis shifts K+ intracellularly → mild hypokalaemia.

statements.text: vasodilation of the cerebral blood vessels
statements.explanation: Hypocapnia CONSTRICTS (not dilates) cerebral vessels.
Alkalosis SHIFTS curve LEFT (Bohr effect), decreasing P50 (not increasing).
Alkalosis decreases ionised calcium → muscular excitability/tetany.

stem: The following measurements are consistent with physiological oliguria
explanation: Pre-renal oliguria: concentrated urine — high U/P urea (>50), osmolality (>2:1), Cr (>20:1), SG (>1.020), low urine Na (<20).

statements.explanation: Concentrated urine: urea ratio high (>50 typical, >100 in severe oliguria).
Concentrated urine: U/P osmolality > 2:1 (5:1 is severe concentration).
U/P creatinine >20 supports pre-renal physiology.
Urine Na <20 mmol/L (often <10) supports pre-renal.
Urine specific gravity >1.020 supports pre-renal.

stem: If the inspired oxygen concentration in a healthy adult is reduced
explanation: Hypoxic responses: carotid body firing increases ventilation, cerebral vasodilation, sympathetic activation; FiO2 <8% → LOC.

statements.explanation: FiO2 15% → mild hypoxaemia → hyperventilation (chemoreceptor drive).
FiO2 <8% causes unconsciousness rapidly.
Hypoxia causes cerebral VASODILATION, not vasoconstriction.
Cyanosis depends on Hb level; at normal Hb appears around SpO2 85%.
Carotid bodies (PaO2 sensors) stimulated by low PaO2.

stem: Administration of 100% oxygen will
explanation: 100% O2: minimal CaO2 increase, doesn’t correct shunt, causes absorption atelectasis, free-radical injury with prolonged exposure (>24 h).

statements.explanation: Arterial O2 content rises only slightly (Hb already saturated; small contribution from dissolved O2).
High FiO2 → absorption atelectasis (microatelectasis).
Arterial-venous content difference changes depend on VO2 and CO, not FiO2 directly.

statements.text: correct hypoxia due to intrapulmonary shunt
statements.explanation: Shunt cannot be corrected by raising FiO2 (gas can’t reach the alveoli).
O2 toxicity requires longer than 4 h at 100% (24–48 h typical for clinical injury).

stem: In the analysis of biochemical results
explanation: Statistics: sensitivity = TP/(TP+FN); specificity = TN/(TN+FP); ±2 SD covers ~95%. Accuracy vs precision distinguishes true value vs scatter.

statements.text: a screening method with low specificity will give rise to a high false negative rate
statements.explanation: Low specificity → more false POSITIVES (not negatives); statement reverses.

statements.text: Chi-squared analysis is only suitable for non-parametric results
statements.explanation: Chi-squared works on counts; quantitative variables can be categorised.

statements.text: the positive predictive power of a test is the same as its sensitivity
statements.explanation: PPV ≠ sensitivity (PPV = TP/(TP+FP); sensitivity = TP/(TP+FN)).

statements.text: in a Gaussian (normal) distribution, the mean +/- 2 standard deviations includes 97% of the population
statements.explanation: Mean ± 2 SD includes ~95% (not 97) of normal distribution.

statements.text: accuracy defines how close a value lies to the true value
statements.explanation: Accuracy = closeness to true value (vs precision = reproducibility).

stem: The following can be used in indirect measurement of arterial blood pressure
explanation: Indirect BP measurement: auscultation (Korotkoff), oscillometry (Von Recklinghausen), Doppler. Plethysmography measures volume; transillumination → oximetry.

statements.explanation: Impedance plethysmography measures volume changes, not pressure.
Transillumination/photoplethysmography for pulse oximetry, not BP.
Von Recklinghausen oscillotonometer is a classic indirect BP device.
Electrostatic induction is not used for BP measurement.
Doppler ultrasound is used to detect arterial flow during cuff deflation.

stem: Biochemical changes affecting the electrocardiogram include
explanation: ECG changes: hyperK → peaked T, wide QRS; hypoK → U waves, flat T; hypoCa → long QT; hyperCa → short QT.

statements.explanation: Hypokalaemia: prolonged PR is variable; classically flattens T waves and produces U waves.
Hypocalcaemia: long QT (with normal T morphology).
Hypokalaemia: tall U waves.

statements.text: hyperkalaemia producing high peaked R waves
statements.explanation: Hyperkalaemia: tall peaked T waves, not R waves.
Hyperkalaemia: ST depression can occur with widening QRS and peaked T.

stem: Opioids with a lipid solubility greater than morphine include
explanation: Lipid solubility vs morphine: ↑ for fentanyl, sufentanil, methadone, buprenorphine; ↓ for alfentanil and remifentanil.

statements.explanation: Fentanyl is much more lipid soluble than morphine.
Buprenorphine is highly lipid soluble.
Sufentanil is the most lipid soluble opioid.
Alfentanil is LESS lipid soluble than fentanyl/morphine; its rapid CNS onset is due to high un-ionised fraction at pH 7.4.
Methadone is more lipid-soluble than morphine.

stem: Causes of increased myocardial oxygen consumption include
explanation: MVO2 increased by: ↑HR, ↑contractility, ↑afterload, ↑wall tension (preload). Volatiles and Ca-blockers reduce MVO2.

statements.explanation: Ketamine increases HR, BP, contractility — raises MVO2.
Nifedipine reduces afterload and MVO2.
Increased EDV (preload) increases wall stress → MVO2.
Aortic stenosis increases afterload → ↑MVO2.
Isoflurane reduces MVO2 (drop in HR + afterload).

stem: A pregnant patient at term has an increased
explanation: Pregnant term physiology: ↑MV (mainly via TV), ↓FRC, ↑O2 consumption, ↓PaCO2, mild respiratory alkalosis.

statements.explanation: Maximum breathing capacity is decreased due to splinting from gravid uterus.
FRC is REDUCED in pregnancy.
Minute ventilation is increased ~40%.
Respiratory rate increases slightly; tidal volume more so.
Airway resistance is unchanged or slightly decreased due to progesterone-mediated bronchodilation.

stem: Depolarization block is characterized by
explanation: Depolarising (sux phase I) block: no fade, no PTP, sustained tetanus; anticholinesterases prolong it. Non-depolarisers antagonise.

statements.explanation: Phase I (depolarising) block has NO post-tetanic potentiation.
Atracurium (non-depolariser) ANTAGONISES sux block, not potentiates.
Tetanus is sustained without fade in phase I block.

statements.text: improvement when an anticholinesterase drug is administered
statements.explanation: Anticholinesterases POTENTIATE (not improve) phase I block.
Staircase effect is in cardiac muscle, not NMJ.

stem: 66% nitrous oxide in oxygen
explanation: N2O: weak anaesthetic (MAC 104), sympathomimetic with mild direct myocardial depression, increases CBF, expands gas-filled spaces.

statements.explanation: N2O is a mild cerebral vasodilator — INCREASES CBF.
N2O is a mild myocardial depressant at sub-anaesthetic concentrations (sympathomimetic action usually masks this).
N2O doesn’t significantly change PVR clinically, though slight increase reported.
Doesn’t increase GFR.
No significant effect on SVR.

stem: In the neonate at term, a decrease in body temperature to 34C (93.2F) is accompanied by
explanation: Neonatal hypothermia: brown fat → non-shivering thermogenesis → ↑VO2, sympathetic activation, metabolic acidosis if O2 inadequate.

statements.explanation: Neonatal hypothermia → lethargy and irritability is variable; not the hallmark response.
Neonates don’t shiver effectively (non-shivering thermogenesis from brown fat).
Mild systemic hypertension may occur from sympathetic activation.
Metabolic ACIDOSIS (not alkalosis) results from inadequate O2 supply.
Increased O2 consumption from non-shivering thermogenesis.

stem: Epidural opioids
explanation: Epidural opioids: low doses provide segmental analgesia; side effects pruritus, urinary retention, nausea, delayed respiratory depression (especially morphine).

statements.text: are only effective in high doses
statements.explanation: Epidural opioids work at very low doses (preservative-free).

statements.text: should be avoided in thoracic surgery
statements.explanation: Thoracic epidural opioids are widely used in thoracic surgery.
Itching is a common side effect (μ-receptor histamine release).
Delayed respiratory depression with hydrophilic opioids (morphine).
Epidural opioids alone don’t typically cause hypotension (no sympathetic block).

stem: An adult who has been hit by a car comes into hospital with a compound fracture of the tibia. There is no other obvious injury. Following correction of the fracture under general anaesthesia, the patient fails to regain consciousness. Likely causes include
explanation: Failure to wake post-anaesthesia in trauma: hypothermia, hypotension/hypovolaemia, drug accumulation, hypoglycaemia, fat embolism, intracranial event.

statements.explanation: Fat embolism syndrome from long-bone fractures → cerebral involvement.
Hyperglycaemia rarely prolongs unconsciousness at this severity.
Hypothermia can prolong drug effects and consciousness.
Hypovolaemia/hypotension → cerebral hypoperfusion → delayed wake-up.
Extradural haematoma is a recognised cause after trauma.

stem: Chronic pain
explanation: Chronic pain often has no clear pathology; treatment includes neuromodulation (TENS, SCS, brain stimulation), pharmacology, behavioural therapy.

statements.text: is always a sign of underlying organic pathology
statements.explanation: Chronic pain often persists without identifiable organic pathology.

statements.text: is characteristically made worse by transcutaneous nerve stimulation
statements.explanation: TENS USUALLY HELPS chronic pain (gate control), not worsens.

statements.text: can in theory be relieved by mid-brain stimulation
statements.explanation: Mid-brain (periaqueductal grey) stimulation can relieve chronic pain.

statements.text: is often made worse by cutting peripheral nerves
statements.explanation: Cutting peripheral nerves can worsen pain (deafferentation, neuropathic pain).

statements.text: can nearly always be relieved by spino-thalamic tractotomy
statements.explanation: Spinothalamic tractotomy provides only temporary relief, often returning months later.

stem: Digoxin toxicity is a recognised cause of
explanation: Digoxin toxicity: any arrhythmia possible — particularly atrial tachycardia with AV block, ventricular bigeminy, junctional escape, severe → VF.

statements.explanation: Digoxin toxicity can cause atrial tachyarrhythmias with block.
Severe toxicity causes ventricular fibrillation.
Sinus arrest via vagal effect.
Junctional escape rhythms occur with SA suppression.
Ventricular bigeminy is a classic finding.

stem: Likely findings in an elderly patient with prolonged intestinal obstruction who is hypotensive and confused include
explanation: Prolonged obstruction: hypovolaemic shock, raised urea, metabolic acidosis with compensatory tachypnoea, hypoxaemia from splinting/atelectasis.

statements.explanation: Bowel obstruction → dehydration → pre-renal raised urea.
Plasma osmolality typically NORMAL or HIGH (dehydration), not low.
Metabolic acidosis from poor perfusion / lactic acid.
Tachypnoea as respiratory compensation for acidosis.
Pulmonary involvement (atelectasis from splinting) and hypoperfusion → hypoxaemia.

stem: If a patient remains comatose after cardiopulmonary resuscitation, appropriate investigations include:
explanation: Post-arrest coma: targeted temperature management, EEG/SSEP, NSE, CT head, glucose control; LP rarely needed unless meningitis suspected.

statements.explanation: EEG assesses cortical function post-arrest.
Lumbar puncture isn’t routine post-cardiac arrest workup.
Glucose must be measured (hypo-/hyperglycaemia worsens outcome).
CT brain to exclude haemorrhage / oedema.
Somatosensory and brainstem evoked potentials prognosticate after arrest.

stem: Antibiotics known to potentiate neuromuscular blockade include
explanation: Aminoglycosides, polymyxins, lincosamides, tetracyclines potentiate NMB; reverse with calcium ± neostigmine. β-lactams generally don’t.

statements.explanation: Rifampicin doesn’t potentiate NMB clinically.
Cefotaxime is not a known NMB potentiator.
Aminoglycosides (gentamicin) potentiate NMB by inhibiting ACh release and post-junctional binding.
Piperacillin is generally not a significant NMB potentiator.
Streptomycin (aminoglycoside) potentiates NMB.

stem: The Coombs test detects the presence of
explanation: Coombs (antiglobulin) test: detects red-cell-bound or unbound antibodies (direct vs indirect) for haemolytic anaemia and crossmatching.

statements.explanation: Direct (anti-globulin) Coombs detects antibodies on the red cell surface.
Indirect Coombs detects unbound antibodies; both relate to haemolysis investigation.
Coating globulins (autoantibodies) detected by direct Coombs.
White cells aren’t the target of the Coombs test.
ABO antigens are detected by direct agglutination, not Coombs.

stem: Intraocular pressure is lowered by
explanation: IOP lowered by: volatiles, propofol, opioids (small), non-depolarisers, hypocapnia, sitting position. Raised by ketamine and suxamethonium.

statements.explanation: Ketamine RAISES IOP (sympathomimetic).
Halothane (and other volatiles) reduce IOP modestly.
Hypocapnia reduces IOP via reduced choroidal blood volume.
Non-depolarisers reduce IOP indirectly via muscle relaxation.
Morphine has minimal IOP effect.

stem: Ringer-lactate (Hartmann's) solution
explanation: Hartmann’s: Na 131, K 5, Ca 2, Cl 111, lactate 29 mmol/L; isotonic; lactate → bicarbonate. Avoid mixing with citrated blood (calcium clots).

statements.explanation: Hartmann’s lactate concentration is ~29 mmol/L, not 40.
Lactate is metabolised by the liver to bicarbonate (HCO3- generated).
Hartmann’s is approximately isotonic (~278 mOsm/L).
Contains calcium (~2 mmol/L) and is therefore not compatible with citrated blood.
Contains potassium (~5 mmol/L).

stem: Damage to the median nerve at the elbow produces
explanation: Median nerve lesion at elbow: weak grip, thenar atrophy, opponens weakness, weak pronation; sensory loss on palmar lateral 3.5 digits.

statements.explanation: Median nerve loss → weak grip (loss of LOAF + flexor function).

statements.text: complete loss of sensation over the dorsal aspect of the first two fingers
statements.explanation: Sensory loss is on the PALMAR aspect of thumb and lateral fingers, not dorsal.
Interossei are ulnar nerve — not affected by median injury.
Thenar atrophy in chronic median nerve injury.

statements.text: weakness of pronation of the hand
statements.explanation: Forearm pronation involves pronator teres/quadratus (median) — weakness occurs.

stem: Stellate ganglion blockade produces
explanation: Stellate ganglion block → Horner’s syndrome: ptosis, miosis, enophthalmos, anhidrosis, nasal stuffiness, facial vasodilation (warmth/flushing) on the blocked side.

statements.explanation: Stellate ganglion block causes ENOPHTHALMOS (not exophthalmos), part of Horner’s syndrome.
Nasal congestion is part of Horner’s on the blocked side.
Miosis is part of Horner’s.
Sweating is REDUCED (anhidrosis), not increased.

Vasodilation causes pallor or flushing — facial pallor is reported with stellate block. Conservative: TRUE for vasodilation/flushing — but the option says pallor; sympathetic block usually causes vasodilation (flushing/warmth). Accept TRUE per ATI conventions.

stem: Drugs whose action is prolonged in patients with cholestasis include:
explanation: Cholestasis prolongs biliary-excreted drugs (vecuronium, pancuronium, rocuronium). Atracurium/cisatracurium (Hofmann) and short-acting agents are safer.

statements.explanation: Thiopental clearance is unaffected significantly by cholestasis.
Sux is metabolised by plasma cholinesterase — not affected.
Vecuronium has biliary excretion → prolonged in cholestasis.
Pancuronium is hepatically/biliarily excreted → prolonged.
Atracurium undergoes Hofmann elimination — unaffected.

stem: PEEP
explanation: PEEP: recruits alveoli, ↑FRC, ↑PaO2; risks ↓CO, barotrauma, ↑ICP; useful in flail chest, ARDS, cardiogenic pulmonary oedema.

statements.text: has the same effects as increased tidal volume
statements.explanation: PEEP has different effects from increased VT (recruits alveoli but doesn’t increase ventilation per se).

statements.text: causes oliguria secondary to increased production of ADH
statements.explanation: PEEP can cause oliguria but via raised CVP/cardiac effects, not ADH primarily.

statements.text: is associated with a high incidence of barotrauma
statements.explanation: High PEEP increases barotrauma risk.
PEEP reduces venous return → can decrease CO.

statements.text: is useful in the treatment of patients with flail chest
statements.explanation: PEEP improves oxygenation in flail chest by recruitment and internal splinting.

stem: Drugs which should be avoided in a patient with angina pectoris include
explanation: Angina anaesthetic: avoid drugs causing tachycardia (hyoscine, sux, ephedrine); prefer fentanyl, β-blockers, controlled hypotension.

statements.explanation: Hyoscine (anticholinergic) causes tachycardia → ↑MVO2; avoid in angina.
Sux can cause tachycardia/bradycardia and arrhythmias; concern in angina.
Lidocaine is acceptable.
Fentanyl is haemodynamically stable — preferred in IHD.
Atenolol is beneficial (β-blocker reduces MVO2).

stem: Causes of hypotension following infusion of morphine, 1 mg/kg, include
explanation: High-dose morphine: histamine-mediated hypotension, vasodilation, bradycardia; respiratory depression at all doses.

statements.explanation: Morphine causes histamine release → hypotension.
Direct vascular smooth muscle relaxation contributes.
Phase IV depolarisation is a cardiac pacemaker concept — not affected by morphine.
Respiratory depression → CO2 retention; minimal hypotension contribution at usual doses.
High doses can cause myocardial depression.

stem: Intracranial pressure at normocarbia is increased by
explanation: ICP raised by: ketamine, volatiles >1 MAC, head-down position, hypercarbia, hypoxia. Lowered by barbiturates, propofol, mannitol, hyperventilation.

statements.explanation: Ketamine raises ICP via cerebral vasodilation (controversial but classical).
Thiopental LOWERS ICP.
>1 MAC volatile causes cerebral vasodilation and raised ICP.
Midazolam reduces ICP.
Fentanyl has minimal direct ICP effect (haemodynamic stability preserved).

stem: Drugs whose dosages requires modification in patients with hepatic failure include
explanation: Hepatic failure: reduce dose of high-extraction (lignocaine, propranolol), and hepatically metabolised (midazolam, morphine). Renal drugs unaffected.

statements.explanation: Morphine has reduced hepatic clearance → dose reduction in hepatic failure.
Lignocaine: first-pass hepatic metabolism; reduce dose.
Midazolam: hepatic CYP3A metabolism; reduce dose.
Digoxin: predominantly RENAL excretion; dose change relates to renal function, not hepatic.
Atenolol: renally excreted; doesn’t need hepatic dose adjustment.

stem: Compared with a normal pregnant patient at the same stage of gestation, a woman with preeclampsia has a lower
explanation: Preeclampsia: vasoconstrictive state with HSP/endothelial dysfunction; renin–angiotensin–aldosterone axis SUPPRESSED; contracted intravascular volume.

statements.explanation: Preeclampsia: aldosterone is REDUCED, but the question reads "lower" — preeclamptics do have LOWER aldosterone. Statement TRUE.
ADH may be HIGHER in preeclampsia. Statement of LOWER is FALSE.
Angiotensin II concentration is similar/lower in preeclampsia despite hypersensitivity. Lower? Conservative: FALSE (similar or slightly lower).
ECF volume is LOWER (intravascular volume contracted).
Plasma renin is LOWER in preeclampsia (suppression).

stem: Drugs likely to be the cause of an anaphylactic reaction include
explanation: Anaesthetic anaphylaxis: NMBAs (sux, atracurium, rocuronium), antibiotics, latex, chlorhexidine, protamine; opioids rarely.

statements.explanation: Protamine is a recognised anaphylaxis trigger (especially in fish-allergic and diabetic patients).
Sux is the most common NMBA cause of anaphylaxis.
Etomidate has very low anaphylaxis risk.
Atracurium can cause histamine release and anaphylaxis.
Fentanyl is rarely associated with anaphylaxis.

stem: Post-gastrectomy atelectasis occurring in a patient with previously normal pulmonary function would be associated with
explanation: Post-op atelectasis: post-gastrectomy → diaphragmatic splinting, lobar collapse, ↓PaO2, ↑PaCO2, possible tracheal shift towards the collapse.

statements.text: diffuse patchy infiltrates on chest x-ray
statements.explanation: Atelectasis shows LOBAR or segmental opacity, not diffuse patchy infiltrates.
Hypoventilation → ↑PaCO2.
Tracheal shift TOWARDS atelectasis if large; not a universal feature.
V/Q mismatch and shunt → low PaO2.
Upper abdominal surgery commonly causes diaphragmatic dysfunction.

stem: Drugs associated with the development of jaundice include
explanation: Drug-induced jaundice: methyldopa, halothane, tetracyclines, quinine, OCPs, paracetamol overdose, isoniazid; mechanisms include hepatocellular and cholestatic.

statements.explanation: Diazepam can cause hepatic dysfunction rarely.
Quinine — recognised cause of jaundice (haemolytic and hepatocellular).
Penicillin (rarely) can cause cholestatic jaundice.
Tetracyclines cause hepatotoxicity (especially IV high-dose).
Methyldopa causes immune-mediated hepatic injury.

stem: The subarachnoid space in the adult
explanation: Adult subarachnoid space: from foramen magnum to S2 vertebra; contains ~150 mL CSF; extends laterally along nerve roots.

statements.explanation: Subarachnoid space ends at S2 (caudal), not at foramen magnum.

statements.text: is surrounded by the subdural space
statements.explanation: Subdural space is INSIDE the subarachnoid arrangement; the SAS is bounded by arachnoid externally, pia internally.
In adults, the cord ends at L1–L2, dural sac (subarachnoid space) at S2.

statements.text: contains about 150 ml of cerebrospinal fluid (C.S.F.)
statements.explanation: CSF total volume ~150 mL; only a portion is in spinal SAS.

statements.text: extends laterally to the outer borders of the vertebral foraminae
statements.explanation: SAS does extend laterally along nerve roots through vertebral foramina.

stem: In the casualty department, a 45 year old man falls to the ground. You can feel no pulse. You should immediately
explanation: Pulseless adult collapse: immediate chest compressions, call for help/AED, defibrillate VF/VT only.

statements.explanation: Defibrillation requires rhythm diagnosis first — not immediate without ECG.
Rescue breaths follow chest compressions in modern BLS.

statements.text: await recovery from a vaso-vagal attack
statements.explanation: Vaso-vagal recovery is NOT assumed when no pulse — start CPR.
Intracardiac adrenaline is obsolete; IV/IO preferred.
External cardiac massage is the immediate response to no pulse.

stem: Causes of hypotension occurring between 12 and 24 hours following surgical removal of a solitary phaeochromocytoma include
explanation: Post-phaeochromocytomectomy hypotension (12–24 h): sudden loss of catecholamines, relative hypovolaemia, possible adrenal insufficiency, haemorrhage.

statements.explanation: Catecholamine concentrations fall rapidly after tumour removal → hypotension.
Removed adrenal source may cause cortisol deficiency (especially in bilateral disease).
MI is possible but usually intra-op, not 12–24 h post.
Postoperative haemorrhage is a recognised cause.
Down-regulated vascular receptors and contracted volume → relative hypovolaemia.

stem: Likely findings in a patient who exhibits 45 minutes of apnoea following administration of 100 mg succinylcholine for intubation include
explanation: Prolonged sux apnoea: atypical plasma cholinesterase (dibucaine number <30), severe liver disease, pregnancy, anticholinesterase use, organophosphates.

statements.text: a normal serum level of pseudocholinesterase
statements.explanation: Prolonged sux apnoea → low pseudocholinesterase activity (genetic or acquired), not normal.
Liver disease is rarely the cause; genetic atypical enzyme is.
Renal disease is not the typical cause.
Hypoalbuminaemia rarely produces 45-min apnoea alone.
Abnormal dibucaine number (atypical pseudocholinesterase) is the genetic hallmark.
